upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows an extremely stable, nearly isothermal layer from the surface to 850mb (13°C at surface, 12.6°C at 1529m), with a lapse rate of only 0.26°C/km versus the dry adiabatic 9.8°C/km — this is a strong subsidence inversion capping thermals well below 850mb. The 850mb dewpoint spread of 15°C confirms dry air aloft, but the near-surface stability means thermals will be weak, disorganized, and likely capped below 1000–1200m AGL at best. Winds aloft are light and variable (SSE at 850mb, veering to SW at 500mb), with no meaningful shear hazard but also no strong directional flow to drive ridge lift.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area. METARs show calm, benign conditions: KSFO calm/10SM with a few high clouds, KAPC calm/10SM CLR, KWVI calm/10SM CLR. No KHAF METAR available to directly assess marine layer depth, but NDBC buoy 46012 (Half Moon Bay) shows SST 13.2°C and buoy 46026 (SF Bar) shows 13.3°C — cold SST supporting a persistent marine layer at coastal sites. KAPC sensor flagged ($) but data appears valid. Overall no aviation hazards.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge, SW/NW-facing, thermal and ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's high launch at 1750ft generates thermal flying when valley heating produces a valley-breeze cycle; ideal wind is NW to W at 8–15 kts for ridge soaring. The lower 600ft launch is more thermal-dependent with lighter wind requirements.
sites.todaySetup: The dominant story today at Ed Levin high launch is 100% cloud cover persisting all day with zero thermal development, completely suppressing what would otherwise be a flyable site. The KOAK sounding's extreme low-level stability (lapse rate 0.26°C/km) confirms thermals are capped, and CAPE is zero throughout. Surface winds are light and variable all morning (2–4 kts, drifting between S and N), then shift NW at 5–6 kts by 2–4 PM — barely enough for ridge lift even on a clear day. Tomorrow.io confirms gusts stay below 7 kts all afternoon with winds from variable directions. The BL height peaks at 1535m around 3 PM but this is likely a model artifact under overcast — cloud cover at 100% through sunset forecloses any solar-driven lift. There is simply no mechanism for flyable conditions today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Overcast at 100%, winds 2–4 kts from SSE/variable. BL only 65–620m. No thermal or ridge potential whatsoever.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Still 100% overcast, winds remain light (2–5 kts) from variable N/NW direction. BL expanding on paper but cloud deck prevents solar heating. Non-flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds clock to NW 5–6 kts by 3–4 PM, briefly reaching 8 kts NW at 5 PM before dropping. Clouds remain 100% all afternoon per both HRRR and GFS. Still no viable window.

verdict.hazards: 100% overcast all day suppresses thermals; strong low-level inversion; winds too light and variable for ridge soaring

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — low launch, SW-facing, thermal-primary site
sites.howItWorks: The 600ft launch at Ed Levin relies almost entirely on thermal lift from valley heating below; ideal conditions require clear skies and light W-NW winds 5–12 kts. Ridge lift is marginal at this elevation.
sites.todaySetup: Like the high launch, the 600ft site is completely shut down today by persistent 100% cloud cover throughout the day. Without solar heating reaching the valley floor, there is zero thermal development, and the extreme surface-to-850mb inversion confirmed by KOAK ensures any thermals that do form will be weak and immediately capped. Surface winds at this lower elevation are even lighter than the high launch — mostly 1–5 kts from variable directions (W-SW in late morning, shifting toward NW afternoon). Tomorrow.io shows gusts peaking at only 5.2 kts, confirming no punchy conditions but also no usable lift. The BL height reaches 1730m at 4 PM per model, but this is irrelevant under solid overcast.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Near-calm, variable winds 0–2.5 kts, 100% overcast from 10 AM onward. BL only 135–460m. Completely unsuitable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds very light W-SW 2–4 kts at surface, 100% cloud cover unchanged. No thermal trigger possible under overcast.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds slowly increase to NW 6–10 kts surface by 5 PM but overcast remains solid per all models. No flyable conditions.

verdict.hazards: Solid overcast all day kills all thermal potential; extreme low-level inversion; insufficient wind for ridge lift at 600ft

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal ridge, W-facing, ridge-soaring site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge-soaring site powered by onshore marine/sea-breeze flow from the W-WNW. Ideal wind is 10–20 kts from 270–300°; too light means sinking out, too strong means rotor and lee turbulence. Marine layer depth affects visibility and cloud base height.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock has a usable afternoon window today, but it's a marginal call requiring experienced judgment. The key issue is that winds build slowly from nearly calm this morning to a workable 6–9 kts W-WNW by afternoon — right on the lower edge of the usable ridge-soaring band. Both HRRR and GFS agree on winds reaching 7–8 kts from 270–285° by 3–4 PM, with 80m winds at 9 kts confirming a west-facing ridge will see better flow at launch height. However, 100% overcast closes in by noon and persists through the evening, and Tomorrow.io shows surface winds well below HRRR estimates (2–3 kts), raising some uncertainty. Cold SST at 13.2°C (buoy 46012) and 13.3°C (buoy 46026) confirm a robust marine layer that will keep the cloud deck low and possibly limit visibility. The morning is definitely not flyable; the afternoon window at 2–5 PM is marginal, best for P3 pilots who can manage a low-end ridge day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Near-calm, 1–2 kts from variable directions, cloud cover building from 4–50%. No ridge lift possible — do not launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds slowly building to 4–5 kts WNW by noon, 100% overcast. Still borderline — marginal ridge lift at best for the most experienced P3 pilots.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: 2–5 PM with 7–8 kts from 270–285° at surface, 9 kts at 80m. 100% overcast limits ceiling but ridge soaring may be possible. Tomorrow.io disagrees with lower values (2–3 kts) so actual conditions may disappoint.

verdict.wind: 7–8 kts W (HRRR); 2–3 kts per Tomorrow.io — model disagreement
verdict.hazards: Model disagreement on wind speed; 100% overcast; cold SST marine layer reducing ceiling; low-end of flyable ridge wind range; Tomorrow.io suggests conditions may be lighter than HRRR predicts

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge/thermal site, W-NW facing, strong thermal potential on good days
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak generates strong thermals on clear inland days with valley heating; it also benefits from the afternoon sea-breeze gradient. Ideal winds are NW 8–18 kts; the site is known for strong, punchy thermals and should be treated as P3+ terrain.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak is grounded today for the same fundamental reason as Ed Levin — 100% overcast throughout the day eliminates solar heating and thermal development. The KOAK sounding's extreme low-level inversion (0.26°C/km lapse rate) means even partial clearing would produce only weak, capped thermals. Winds are light and variable all morning (2–5 kts from SSE, near calm mid-morning), shifting to NW 5–6 kts in the afternoon — marginally in the right direction but far too light for any useful ridge lift at this site. Tomorrow.io does show a brief 6.8 kt SE gust at 2 PM, but that's cross or tail wind to the launch, not useful. Overcast persists at 100% all day per both HRRR and GFS with no clearing signal.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 4–5 kts SSE shifting to near-calm then variable. 100% overcast from 10 AM. No thermal or ridge conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds variable 2–5 kts, 100% overcast, CAPE zero. BL height expanding per models but irrelevant under cloud deck.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds shift NW 5–8 kts by 3–5 PM with 80m winds at 6–9 kts. Still 100% overcast — no thermal trigger available. Not flyable.

verdict.hazards: 100% overcast all day; extreme low-level inversion suppresses thermals; winds insufficient for ridge soaring; site known for punchy conditions even on marginal days

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Monday looks significantly better. GFS/HRRR show clearing by late morning (clouds dropping from 88% to 0% by noon), with W-WNW winds building to 7–8 kts by noon and 8–9 kts by 2–4 PM from 287–291°. BL height reaches 305–335m — modest but improving. However Tomorrow.io shows much lighter winds (2–4 kts) and 100% overcast all day, a significant model disagreement. Weight HRRR more heavily but treat as marginal — morning cloud burn-off timing is the key uncertainty. Could be a decent P2/P3 afternoon if HRRR is right.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Monday shows similar improvement: skies clearing by midday, W-NW winds 6–10 kts from 296–314° by afternoon, BL 240–350m. Tomorrow.io again shows much lighter winds (3–7 kts) with persistent overcast — significant model spread. If HRRR verifies, the 600ft site could see a workable thermal/ridge afternoon window around 12–4 PM. Marginal P2 if cloud burn-off happens; N/A if Tomorrow.io is correct.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Monday looks strong at Mussel Rock, possibly the best day of the week. Both HRRR and GFS agree on sustained W-WNW winds of 10–12 kts surface (13–15 kts at 80m) from 281–292° throughout the day, persisting from morning through evening. Tomorrow.io confirms with 8–12 kts and gusts to 11–12 kts. 100% overcast but that's irrelevant for ridge soaring. This looks like a solid ridge day. P3 pilots, watch for strong flow — gusts to 12 kts with 15 kts at altitude may make it a P3+ day.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Monday looks marginal to possibly flyable depending on cloud burn-off. Winds build to W-NW 7–8 kts by afternoon (similar to Ed Levin), but Tomorrow.io shows very light winds and persistent overcast — same model disagreement as Ed Levin. If HRRR verifies and skies clear, a P3 afternoon window around 1–4 PM is possible. Do not count on it.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday (Apr 7) shows light morning winds from SE going calm, then a NW sea breeze building to 8–10 kts by 2–4 PM from 311–312°. BL reaches 925–940m with 75–100% cloud cover afternoon — limiting factor. Potential for afternoon thermal/ridge soaring if cloud cover doesn't completely shut down heating. Watch the cloud trend.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday mirrors the high launch pattern — light SE morning, NW 8–10 kts afternoon, BL 820–940m. Cloud cover builds to 75–100% by afternoon which again caps thermal development. Similar marginal/possible outlook to Monday.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Tuesday looks like another ridge day at Mussel Rock. Winds 7–11 kts W from 268–274°, building through the afternoon. 95–100% overcast. Solid W-facing flow should produce good ridge lift, though gusts to 12 kts at 80m warrant P3 attention.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Tuesday shows W-WNW winds building 5–6 kts surface, 10–14 kts at 80m by 2–4 PM — interesting wind shear between surface and altitude. BL 820–855m with 79–100% cloud cover afternoon. The shear between 6 kts surface and 14 kts at 80m is a turbulence flag. P3+ conditions if clouds permit any thermal trigger.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 (Apr 8) data not yet in the model window — extrapolate from the pattern: the NW afternoon sea breeze cycle appears to be establishing. If the marine layer continues its morning pattern of late burn-off, expect similar marginal afternoon windows.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Same as high launch for Day 3 — insufficient data beyond Apr 7 in current models. The pattern suggests continued afternoon NW flow potential.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Day 3 insufficient data, but the persistent W-NW marine flow pattern suggests Mussel Rock remains the most reliable site in the region. If the pressure gradient holds, another ridge day is plausible.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Day 3 beyond current model confidence. If the Tuesday wind shear pattern continues, Mission Peak warrants caution. Monitor updated soundings for shear between surface and 80m.

watchlist: Model disagreement between HRRR/GFS and Tomorrow.io is significant today and tomorrow — HRRR shows usable winds at Mussel Rock afternoon, Tomorrow.io shows near-calm. Verify with morning METARs before driving to site.
100% overcast at all inland sites (Ed Levin, Mission Peak) today is the primary no-fly trigger — any unexpected clearing would dramatically change the picture.
Cold SST (13.2–13.3°C) at buoys 46012 and 46026 indicates a robust, persistent marine layer — coastal sites should expect late burn-off or no burn-off all week.
Monday at Mussel Rock: winds forecast 10–15 kts with gusts — review actual morning METARs at KSFO and KHAF before flying; may be P3+ strength.
KAPC sensor flagged ($) — Napa automated station may have calibration issues; cross-reference with KSFO for inland gradient assessment.
tldr: Today is essentially a no-fly day at all thermal sites (Ed Levin, Mission Peak) due to solid overcast killing thermals, with a marginal ridge window at Mussel Rock from 2–5 PM if HRRR's 7–8 kt W winds verify over Tomorrow.io's lighter estimate. Tomorrow looks considerably better — Mussel Rock in particular shapes up for a solid P3 ridge day with 10–12 kts W. If you're driving somewhere today, Mussel Rock in the afternoon is your only real shot, P3 only.
